digest.top_line: PEACE-2: elective pelvic RT misses cPFS (HR 0.81, p=0.088), splitting from POP-RT (bPFS HR 0.50); whole-pelvis nodal benefit now contested.
digest.tldr: Prostate led with the elective pelvic-RT question splitting: PEACE-2 null on cPFS against POP-RT's positive biochemical signal. Breast brought long-horizon de-escalation reads (APBI, hypofx, boost/IM-MS omission). OLIGOMA delivered the first positive breast-oligomet MDT RCT (mPFS 35.8 vs 20.4, HR 0.48).

sites.intro: Pelvic nodal RT is on trial from both ends: PEACE-2 negative on efficacy, PACE-NODES quantifying what the nodal volume costs in a 5-fraction regimen.

studies.name: POP-RT vs PEACE-2
studies.tldr: WPRT bFFS HR 0.50 in POP-RT vs bPFS HR 0.97 in PEACE-2; pelvic nodal RT benefit vanishes with 3yr ADT and PSMA staging.

details.text: 📊 Whole-pelvis RT efficacy by trial (HR <1 favors WPRT)

details: 🔍 POP-RT: 24mo ADT, prostate 74-76 Gy EQD2, conventional CT/bone-scan staging
🔍 PEACE-2: 36mo ADT, prostate 78 Gy EQD2, conventional/choline PET/CT staging
🔍 Both phase III, open-label, randomized, WPRT+prostate boost vs prostate-only IMRT, N0M0
🔍 POP-RT accrued 2011-2016 (median f/u 6.3-7.2 yr); PEACE-2 accrued 2018-2023
⚠️ PEACE-2 read is an interim analysis at ESTRO 2026, median f/u ~5.5 yr
⚠️ Populations differ: POP-RT high/very-high risk vs PEACE-2 very-high risk only
⚠️ Cross-trial comparison, not a randomized head-to-head; era and systemic intensification confound the RT question
📊 Both trials: comparable grade ≥2 late GU toxicity between arms, no toxicity penalty for WPRT
studies.significance: The two trials differ on three axes at once, so the null in PEACE-2 cannot be attributed to WPRT alone: 36 vs 24 months ADT, 78 vs 74-76 Gy EQD2 to prostate, and PSMA PET staging that removes occult N1 pts POP-RT would have kept. That confounding is the whole elective-nodal decision.
studies.monday_clinic: In very-high-risk N0M0 pts staged by PSMA PET and committed to 3 years of ADT, this comparison questions routine elective pelvic coverage; it does not speak to pts staged conventionally or planned for shorter ADT, where POP-RT's bFFS benefit still stands.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Two independent **phase III open-label randomized** trials compared side by side in a curator-made graphic, **not a head-to-head**. Both randomized whole-pelvis RT plus prostate boost vs prostate-only IMRT in **N0M0** disease.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: POP-RT enrolled **high / very high-risk** localized prostate cancer; PEACE-2 restricted to **very high-risk** only. PEACE-2 used **PSMA PET/CT** staging, POP-RT conventional CT and bone scan, so occult N1 disease that POP-RT would have carried into its N0 cohort was likely excluded in PEACE-2.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: POP-RT: IMRT whole pelvis plus prostate boost, prostate dose **74-76 Gy EQD2**. PEACE-2: dose-escalated IMRT whole pelvis plus boost, prostate dose **78 Gy EQD2**. Same target-volume question, different dose intensity.

analysis_sections.label: Regimen
analysis_sections.body: ADT duration is the other separating variable: **24 months** (GnRH analog plus antiandrogen) in POP-RT vs **36 months** in PEACE-2.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Primary:** bFFS in POP-RT, biochemical PFS in PEACE-2. Secondary in both: clinical progression-free survival, MFS, OS, toxicity; PEACE-2 adds CSS.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: See the efficacy table. POP-RT positive across bFFS, cFFS, and MFS; PEACE-2 **no significant improvement** in bPFS, MFS, CSS, or OS, with only a borderline cPFS trend.

analysis_sections.label: Safety
analysis_sections.body: Both trials reported **comparable grade ≥2 late GU toxicity** between arms. **No significant increase in toxicity** with whole-pelvis RT in either trial.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents high and very high-risk N0M0 localized prostate cancer treated with definitive IMRT plus long-course ADT. Does not represent node-positive, metastatic, or postoperative salvage settings.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Cross-trial comparison with **three simultaneous confounders** (ADT duration, prostate dose, staging modality), so the WPRT effect cannot be isolated. PEACE-2 is an **interim analysis** at median ~5.5 years vs 6.3-7.2 years in POP-RT, and biochemical endpoints mature earlier than MFS.

analysis_sections.label: Discussion
analysis_sections.body: The graphic's own read is that longer ADT, modern staging, and intensified local therapy **may reduce the incremental benefit** of elective pelvic RT in very high-risk disease. Plausible, but the same data are compatible with stage migration alone: PSMA PET pulls occult N1 pts out of the N0 cohort, removing the very pts most likely to benefit from nodal coverage.

open_questions: Does WPRT benefit persist with PSMA-staged N0 disease?
Is 36mo ADT substituting for elective nodal coverage?
Will mature PEACE-2 follow-up change the MFS read?

studies.name: PRIME
studies.tldr: Interim data: 36.25 Gy/5 fx SBRT with whole-pelvis RT and 2y ADT shows no inferiority signal vs 68 Gy/25 fx; BFFS immature.
details: 🔍 Phase III open-label randomized non-inferiority, Tata Memorial Centre + collaborating Indian centers; accrual 2018-2023, N~434, 1:1 stratified by risk group and nodal status
🔍 Eligibility: high-risk, very high-risk and/or node-positive non-metastatic prostate; ECOG 0-2; life expectancy 10 years; PSMA PET/CT staging allowed
📊 1° EP: BFFS (Phoenix, nadir + 2 ng/mL). Interim only, median f/u 1-2y; mature 4-5y efficacy pending

details.text: 📐 RT prescription, both arms include whole-pelvis elective nodal RT
subdetails: Arm A: 36.25 Gy / 5 fx (7.25 Gy per fx), every other day
Arm B: ~68 Gy / 25 fx (2.7 Gy per fx), 5 fx/week, ~5 weeks
Pelvic nodes: 25 Gy / 5 fx (or equivalent) in both arms
SIB to positive nodes allowed in the SBRT arm
Long-course ADT ~2 years in both arms

details: 📊 Acute grade ≥2 GU ~5.4% SBRT vs ~4.0% moderate hypo, p=0.59; GI ~2.2% vs ~3.7%, p=0.20
📊 Grade 3+ acute and late toxicity <1% in both arms; late grade ≥2 GU ~10-12% vs ~9-11% (NS), GI ~5-7% vs ~4-6% (NS)
📊 QoL: EORTC QLQ-C30 / QLQ-PR25 / IPSS urinary and bowel domains stable and comparable; transient on-treatment declines with recovery

🔗 vs HYPO-RT-PC (ISRCTN45905321): 42.7 Gy/7 fx vs 78 Gy/39 fx, node-negative only, NO pelvic RT and NO ADT, mostly 3D-CRT; 10y FFS 72% vs 65%, adjusted HR 0.84 (95% CI 0.69-1.03), non-inferiority confirmed
⚠️ Efficacy read is interim at 1-2y for a BFFS endpoint under 2y of ADT; testosterone suppression masks biochemical failure, so early curve overlap carries little information
⚠️ 'No signal of inferiority' is a trend statement, not a met non-inferiority margin; no HR, CI, or margin reported in source
⚠️ Toxicity numbers in source are approximate ranges (~5.4%, ~10-12%), not point estimates with CIs; abstract-level reporting

verdict.rationale: Interim analysis at 1-2y of a BFFS primary that needs 4-5y maturity; safety-only read. Interim data cannot promote past early-signal regardless of clean design.
verdict.audience: High-risk, very high-risk and/or node-positive non-metastatic prostate, 2y ADT

studies.significance: The transferable point is the pelvic dose: 25 Gy / 5 fx elective nodal with SIB permitted to involved nodes, grade 3+ under 1%, which is the constraint question that has kept 5-fraction prescribing confined to node-negative prostate-only. HYPO-RT-PC never tested nodes or ADT, so this extends the ultrahypofractionation toxicity envelope, not its efficacy.
studies.monday_clinic: In high-risk or node-positive non-metastatic prostate going to whole-pelvis RT with long-course ADT, this supports 5-fraction SBRT as tolerable but not yet as an efficacy-equivalent substitute; it says nothing about omitting ADT or treating without pelvic coverage.

primary_endpoint.name: Biochemical failure-free survival
primary_endpoint.stat_detail: Interim: no signal of inferiority for SBRT arm; 4-5y data awaited

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: **Phase III, open-label, randomized non-inferiority** trial, Tata Memorial Centre and collaborating Indian centers. Accrual 2018-2023, completed at **N~434**, randomized **1:1** stratified by risk group and nodal status. Median follow-up is **interim, 1-2 years**.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: **High-risk, very high-risk and/or node-positive non-metastatic** prostate cancer; ECOG 0-2; life expectancy 10 years. PSMA PET/CT staging permitted, which matters for how the node-positive stratum was defined relative to older conventionally-staged trials.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: Arm A **36.25 Gy in 5 fractions (7.25 Gy/fx), every other day**; Arm B **~68 Gy in 25 fractions (2.7 Gy/fx)** over ~5 weeks. **Both arms received prostate plus whole-pelvis elective nodal RT at 25 Gy / 5 fx (or equivalent)**, with SIB to positive nodes allowed in the SBRT arm. Delivered in the modern IMRT/VMAT with daily IGRT era.

analysis_sections.label: Regimen
analysis_sections.body: **Long-course ADT, ~2 years, in both arms.** ADT is not a randomized variable here, so the trial isolates fractionation, not the systemic question.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Primary: biochemical failure-free survival** (Phoenix, nadir + 2 ng/mL). Secondary: acute and late toxicity (RTOG/CTCAE v4.0/5.0), OS, MFS, clinical failure-free survival, QoL (QLQ-C30, QLQ-PR25, IPSS), cost-effectiveness.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: Interim only. Source reports **no signal of inferiority** for the SBRT arm on BFFS and no difference in MFS or OS in early follow-up; **no HR, CI, or non-inferiority margin is reported in source**. OS data explicitly immature.

analysis_sections.label: Safety
analysis_sections.body: No significant difference in grade ≥2 toxicity between arms: acute GU ~5.4% vs ~4.0% (p=0.59), acute GI ~2.2% vs ~3.7% (p=0.20); late grade ≥2 GU ~10-12% vs ~9-11% and GI ~5-7% vs ~4-6%, both NS. **Grade 3+ under 1% in both arms, acute and late.** Urinary and bowel QoL domains stable and comparable.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: HYPO-RT-PC established ultrahypofractionation non-inferiority (**10y FFS 72% vs 65%, adjusted HR 0.84, 95% CI 0.69-1.03**) but in **node-negative disease, prostate plus proximal seminal vesicles only, no pelvic RT, no ADT, mostly 3D-CRT**. PRIME is testing a different question: 5 fractions with elective nodal coverage on top of 2y ADT.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents high-risk, very high-risk and/or node-positive non-metastatic prostate cancer treated with whole-pelvis RT and 2 years of ADT. Does not represent intermediate-risk, node-negative, prostate-only, or ADT-omitted patients, nor anyone needing a mature efficacy answer today.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Interim analysis at 1-2 years against a **BFFS primary read under 2 years of ADT**, where testosterone suppression flattens both curves and early concordance is close to uninformative. Toxicity is reported as approximate ranges rather than point estimates with CIs. Open-label, though toxicity grading and PSA endpoints are only modestly susceptible to that.

open_questions: Mature 4-5y BFFS and non-inferiority margin
Late GU toxicity beyond 2y with nodal SIB
Whether pelvic nodal RT itself adds benefit in this population

studies.name: PIVOTALboost
studies.tldr: Late 2yr G2+ bowel 8.2% / 8.7% / 6.5% and bladder 19.5% / 24.1% / 16.5% across prostate, +boost, +pelvic nodes arms.
details: 🔍 Phase 3 RCT, N=1465 randomised, 39 UK centres; 1° EP is biochemical/clinical failure (not reported here)
🔍 Three arms received: prostate IMRT 388, prostate IMRT + prostate boost (P+B) 464, prostate + pelvic IMRT + boost (PPN+B) 462
📐 20-fraction moderate hypofractionation; focal intraprostatic boost; elective pelvic nodal volume in the PPN+B arm
📊 Late toxicity cohort = 973 (74%) pts with ≥2yr follow-up; cumulative G2+ rates at 6-24 months
📊 Nodal RT raised early bowel side effects but resolved by 18 weeks post-RT; no significant 2yr differences
⚠️ Toxicity is a SECONDARY endpoint; efficacy (biochemical/clinical failure) not reported in source, so the boost/nodal benefit question stays open
⚠️ Late analysis restricted to the 74% with 2yr follow-up; no p-values or between-arm comparative statistics reported in source
⚠️ Denominators shift between randomisation (388/464/462) and the late-toxicity cohort (281/345/347); reasons not reported in source
🔗 Complements PIVOTAL (pelvic nodal IMRT feasibility) and the 20-fraction backbone from CHHiP; extends focal-boost safety beyond FLAME's 35-fraction schedule

verdict.rationale: Randomised phase 3, prespecified secondary toxicity endpoint, 2yr follow-up. Efficacy 1° EP not yet reported, so it supports safety only, not a practice change.

studies.significance: The late bladder G2+ signal sits with the boost, not the nodes: 24.1% in P+B vs 19.5% prostate-only and 16.5% in PPN+B, so adding elective nodal volume did not track with worse 2yr bladder or bowel toxicity. That makes the 20-fraction focal boost plus pelvic node volume a tolerable technique choice while the biochemical/clinical failure endpoint matures.
studies.monday_clinic: In high-risk localised prostate pts being planned for 20-fraction IMRT, this supports the safety of adding a focal intraprostatic boost and elective pelvic nodal coverage; it does not yet speak to whether either improves biochemical or clinical control.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: **Phase 3 RCT**, **N=1465 randomised** across **39 UK centres**. **Primary endpoint: biochemical/clinical failure**; the side-effect endpoints presented here are **secondary**.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Localised prostate cancer; the trial rationale states **high-risk localised pts are most likely to benefit** from pelvic node RT. Full eligibility criteria not reported in source.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: **20-fraction** moderately hypofractionated IMRT. Arms as received: **prostate alone (388)**, **prostate + focal prostate boost (464)**, **prostate + pelvic nodes + boost (462)**. Boost dose and nodal dose not reported in source.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: Reported here: **early bowel and bladder side effects (<18 weeks)** and **late (2-year, 6-24 month) G2+ events**. Cancer-outcome follow-up is ongoing.

analysis_sections.label: Safety
analysis_sections.body: Nodal RT **increased early bowel side effects**, which **resolved by 18 weeks** post-RT. At 2 years G2+ bowel and bladder rates were **similar across arms**, with the numerically highest late bladder rate in the boost-without-nodes arm.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents high-risk localised prostate pts treated with 20-fraction IMRT at UK centres. Does not represent pts treated with conventional 35-40 fraction schedules, SBRT, or post-prostatectomy salvage.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Late analysis covers only the **973 (74%)** with ≥2yr follow-up, and **no between-arm p-values** are reported in source. The **efficacy primary endpoint is unreported**, so the toxicity read cannot yet be set against a benefit.

studies.name: PACE-NODES
studies.tldr: Grade ≥2 GI toxicity 28% vs 21% with added pelvic nodal SBRT over 12wk; GU unchanged, symptoms resolved by 12wk.
details: 🔍 Phase 3 randomised, 1:1, N=1166 randomised (target n=1128), multicentre UK
🔍 Eligibility: high-risk localised (T3a-T4 and/or Gleason 8-10 and/or PSA>20), planned 12-36mo ADT
📊 Acute toxicity co-primaries: CTCAE grade ≥2 GI and GU up to 12wk
📐 1° trial endpoint is time to biochemical/clinical failure; explicitly not yet mature
⚠️ 11% of PPN-SBRT vs 4% of P-SBRT did not receive allocated treatment, mostly unmet dose constraints
⚠️ Deliverability gap is itself a signal: nodal volumes push OAR constraints in a 5f regimen
🔗 Nodal RT benefit in high-risk disease established with moderate hypofractionation (per authors); this tests whether 5f delivery is tolerable
⚠️ Acute-only read at 12wk; late GI/GU and cancer-control data pending

figures.caption: Trial schema: 36.25Gy/5f prostate both arms, 25Gy/5f nodes in PPN-SBRT, alternate days. Target n=1128.

verdict.rationale: Randomised, but this is an acute-toxicity readout only; the primary biochemical/clinical failure endpoint is explicitly immature. Safety supports feasibility, not adoption.
verdict.audience: High-risk localised prostate (T3a-T4, Gleason 8-10, or PSA>20), planned 12-36mo…

studies.significance: The deliverability number is the buried read: 11% of PPN-SBRT pts never received the allocated treatment vs 4% prostate-only, mostly unmet constraints. Nodal dose was 25Gy/5f alongside 36.25Gy/5f prostate on alternate days. That gates whether 5f elective nodal coverage is exportable outside high-volume centres, before any efficacy signal arrives.
studies.monday_clinic: In high-risk localised prostate on long-course ADT, this supports 5f nodal SBRT as tolerable in the acute window; it does not yet inform whether to cover nodes, since the biochemical/clinical failure endpoint is immature.

primary_endpoint.name: Acute CTCAE grade ≥2 GI toxicity to 12 weeks
primary_endpoint.stat_detail: PPN-SBRT vs P-SBRT; no difference at 12wk, symptoms resolved quickly

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: **Phase 3 randomised**, 1:1, multicentre. **1166 pts randomised** against a target of 1128. This report is the prespecified **acute toxicity** analysis; the trial's primary endpoint, time to biochemical or clinical failure, is **not yet mature**.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: **High-risk localised prostate cancer**: T3a-T4 and/or **Gleason 8-10** and/or **PSA >20 ng/ml**. All planned for **12-36 months ADT**.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: Both arms **36.25Gy in 5f to prostate on alternate days**. PPN-SBRT adds **25Gy in 5f to pelvic nodes**. **11% of PPN-SBRT** vs 4% of P-SBRT did not receive allocation, mostly because **dose constraints were not met**.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Co-primary for this analysis:** CTCAE grade ≥2 **GI** and **GU** toxicity to 12 weeks. Patient-reported **EPIC-26** domain scores collected at 4 weeks.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: Grade ≥2 GI symptoms were **more frequent with PPN-SBRT (28% vs 21%)** across the 12-week window, with **no difference at 12 weeks**. **No difference in acute GU** toxicity by clinician or patient report. EPIC-26 bowel domain at 4 weeks mirrored the clinician-scored GI gap.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents high-risk localised prostate cancer planned for 12-36 months ADT, treated with 5-fraction SBRT. Does not represent node-positive or metastatic disease, low/intermediate-risk disease, or conventionally fractionated nodal RT.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Acute window only, capped at 12 weeks; **late GI/GU effects and cancer control are still accruing**. Toxicity scoring is unblinded, which matters most for clinician-graded GI events. Effect sizes for the GI difference (CI, p) were not reported in source.

studies.name: PEACE-2
studies.tldr: 7yr cPFS 67.1% pelvic vs 62.9% prostate-only RT, HR 0.81 (0.63-1.03), p=0.088: primary endpoint not met.
details: 🔍 International multicenter randomized trial, 2×2 design: prostate-only vs pelvic RT, ± cabazitaxel ×4, all with ADT ×3yr
🔍 Eligibility: very high-risk localized CaP with ≥2 of Gleason ≥8, T3-T4, PSA ≥20 ng/mL
🔍 N0M0 staging by conventional imaging or choline PET/CT, i.e. pre-PSMA-PET era enrollment
📐 Pelvic RT arm n=381, prostate-only n=380 at risk at randomization
⚠️ HR from multivariable analysis, not the unadjusted stratified estimate; p=0.088 crosses no prespecified threshold
⚠️ Absolute 7yr cPFS difference is 4.2 points, with 95% CIs that overlap substantially (57.4-68.1 vs 61.6-72.2)

⚠️ Presenter's own framing: <1 in 10 men die of prostate cancer in the first decade, which questions whether this "very high-risk" definition selects a population with enough events for whole-pelvis RT to matter
🔗 Directionally opposite to POP-RT (Murthy, JCO 2021), which showed a bFFS benefit for whole-pelvis RT in high-risk N0 disease; PEACE-2 pelvic HR does not reproduce that magnitude

verdict.rationale: Randomised, prespecified primary cPFS, mature 7yr readout, null result against a widely adopted practice. Design internally valid; the divergence is the headline.
verdict.audience: Very high-risk localized N0M0 prostate ca (≥2 of GS≥8, T3-T4, PSA≥20)

studies.significance: The elective pelvic nodal decision in very high-risk N0 disease loses its randomized support: 4.2 absolute points of 7yr cPFS, HR 0.81, CI crossing 1. Staging was conventional or choline PET, so pts PSMA PET would now upstage sat in both arms, diluting any true nodal benefit. Toxicity by arm not reported in source.
studies.monday_clinic: In very high-risk localized N0M0 prostate cancer staged without PSMA PET, this questions routine whole-pelvis coverage over prostate-only RT with 3yr ADT; it does not speak to pts with PSMA-detected nodal disease, who were not enrolled.

primary_endpoint.name: Clinical progression-free survival
primary_endpoint.stat_detail: HR 0.81, 95% CI 0.63-1.03, p=0.088; primary endpoint not met

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: International multicenter randomized trial with **four arms** crossing RT volume (prostate vs pelvis) with cabazitaxel ×4 cycles, all on **ADT ×3 years**. Readout presented at ESTRO 2026 with **7-year outcomes** and follow-up extending to 120 months.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Very high-risk localized prostate cancer defined as **≥2 risk factors** among Gleason ≥8, T3-T4, and PSA ≥20 ng/mL. **N0M0** by conventional imaging or choline PET/CT.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: The randomized comparison is **target volume**: prostate-only versus **pelvic** irradiation. Dose, fractionation, and nodal CTV definition are not reported in the source.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Primary: cPFS.** Secondary: PSA response at 3 months, bPFS, metastasis-free survival, CaP-specific survival, OS, acute and long-term tolerance, QoL, and biopsy biomarkers.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: Pelvic RT did **not** significantly improve cPFS on multivariable analysis. Secondary endpoints including MFS, CaP-specific survival, and OS are not reported in the source.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents very high-risk localized N0M0 prostate cancer staged by conventional imaging or choline PET/CT and treated with long-course ADT. Does not represent pts with PSMA PET-detected nodal or metastatic disease, nor node-positive disease.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Effect estimate is from **multivariable** analysis; the 2×2 cabazitaxel randomization complicates attribution of the RT-volume effect. **RT-attributable GU/GI toxicity by arm is not reported in source**, which is the other half of the elective-nodal decision.

analysis_sections.label: Discussion
analysis_sections.body: The investigators' own conclusion pivots from the RT question to the **definition of "very high-risk"**: with **<1 in 10** men dying of prostate cancer in the first decade, the population may be over-labeled. They call for biomarkers to guide intensification or de-intensification rather than uniform volume escalation.

studies.name: RADIOSA (MFS post-hoc)
studies.tldr: Post-hoc MFS 16.6mo vs not reached, HR 0.39 favoring SBRT + 6mo ADT over SBRT alone in oligorecurrent prostate.
details: 📊 Metastatic progression 32/51 (62.7%) Arm A vs 19/51 (37.3%) Arm B
📊 Median MFS 16.6mo (95% CI 12.83-NA) Arm A, not reached Arm B
🔍 Phase II randomized 1:1, N=102, SBRT alone vs SBRT + 6mo ADT
🔍 Median f/u (reverse KM) 49.23mo (95% CI 42.47-54.8)
📊 Eugonadal MFS (from testosterone recovery) still favored Arm B, p<0.05

subdetails: MFS was not the trial's primary endpoint; this analysis is post-hoc
All but two Arm B pts recovered testosterone, so eugonadal set is near-complete but unstated N
No OS data reported in source; MFS used as surrogate

details: ⚠️ No SBRT dose, fractionation, or target volume reported in source
🔗 Consistent in direction with STOMP / ORIOLE-era MDT data, but those tested MDT vs observation, not MDT ± ADT

figures.caption: Methods: N=102 randomized 1:1; median follow-up 49.23 months (95% CI 42.47-54.8).

verdict.rationale: Post-hoc endpoint analysis of a phase II trial; MFS was not the prespecified primary. Design dominates the read despite the clean randomisation and mature follow-up.
verdict.audience: Oligorecurrent prostate cancer eligible for metastasis-directed SBRT

studies.significance: The additive read is the eugonadal MFS: benefit persisted after testosterone recovery (p<0.05), so the ADT effect is not just on-treatment suppression of imaging progression. That argues against reading RADIOSA's MFS split as a testosterone artifact, and moves the SBRT-alone vs SBRT + short-course ADT decision in oligorecurrence.
studies.monday_clinic: In oligorecurrent prostate cancer being considered for metastasis-directed SBRT, this supports the discussion of adding 6-month ADT over SBRT alone; it does not address de novo metastatic or castration-resistant disease, and the SBRT dose and target volume are not stated in the source.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Phase II randomized trial, **1:1**, **N=102**, Arm A **SBRT alone** vs Arm B **SBRT + 6-month ADT**. Median follow-up (reverse KM) **49.23 months** (95% CI 42.47-54.8). This report is a **post-hoc** analysis of MFS and eugonadal MFS.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: **Oligorecurrent prostate cancer**. Detailed eligibility (number of lesions, imaging modality, prior local therapy, PSA thresholds) not reported in source.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: **SBRT** to the oligorecurrent sites in both arms. **Dose, fractionation, and target volume are not reported in source**, which limits transfer to a specific practice.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **MFS** defined as randomisation to any M1 metastatic recurrence on imaging. **Eugonadal MFS** measured from **testosterone recovery** to new metastasis or last follow-up. KM curves compared by log-rank; HRs from Cox models.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: Effect sizes are in the figure caption table. All Arm B pts except two reached testosterone recovery within follow-up.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents oligorecurrent prostate cancer treated with metastasis-directed SBRT with or without 6-month ADT. Does not represent de novo metastatic, castration-resistant, or polymetastatic disease.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: **Post-hoc** analysis; MFS was not the prespecified primary endpoint. **No OS reported** in source, so the surrogate carries the read. Toxicity and SBRT technique parameters absent from source.

analysis_sections.label: Discussion
analysis_sections.body: The eugonadal analysis is the substantive contribution: separating the benefit from on-treatment castration addresses the standing objection that ADT simply delays imaging-detected progression. Whether that reflects **durable synergy** between ablation and transient androgen suppression, as the authors argue, is hypothesis-generating at N=102.

⚠️ No OS signal reported in source (listed as secondary); PFS in a systemic-therapy-driven disease is a soft endpoint for a local intervention
🔗 First RCT to show a PFS benefit from MDT specifically in oligometastatic breast, per the presenter's own discussion slide
🔗 Eight further RCTs still accruing per the discussion slide (TAORMINA, STEREO-SEIN, LARA, CLEAR, COSMO, ARCHER, ISTMET, OLIGAMI)

verdict.rationale: Randomised but stopped early at <20% of initial accrual target; N=87 with wide HR CI (0.25-0.91). Underpowered for a durable PFS estimate.
verdict.audience: Oligometastatic breast, ≤5 lesions, any line, mostly ER+/HER2- first line

studies.significance: The RT read is who was actually irradiated: 2/3 of lesions were bone and >80% of pts had 1-3 mets, so this is largely bone-directed ablative RT in first-line ER+ disease, not a visceral-oligomet population. Dose and fractionation are not reported in source, which gates transfer to practice. QoL non-inferiority at 12wk (-2.1, margin -10) removes the main argument against adding local therapy to a working systemic backbone.
studies.monday_clinic: In ER+/HER2- breast cancer with 1-3 mostly bony metastases on first-line systemic therapy, this supports discussing ablative RT to all lesions as a PFS-directed addition; it does not extend to >5 lesions, heavily visceral disease, or pts needing palliative RT to every site.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Randomised trial (**ARO-2021-09**, NCT04495309) of systemic therapy alone vs systemic therapy **plus local ablative radiotherapy to all metastatic lesions**. Randomisation stratified by type of systemic therapy and treatment line. **N=87** (43 experimental, 44 control).

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Metastatic breast cancer, **any treatment line**, **maximum 5 metastatic lesions**, with the systemic regimen determined by multidisciplinary tumour board. Pts requiring palliative RT to **all** metastases were **not eligible**, though palliative RT to symptomatic sites was allowed. Median age 58 / 59; ECOG 0 in 76.7% of the experimental arm.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: The intervention is **local ablative radiotherapy to all metastatic lesions** on top of the systemic backbone. **Dose, fractionation, modality, and target-volume definition are not reported in the source slides**, and 2/3 of treated lesions were bone metastases.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Co-primary:** progression-free survival and quality of life (EORTC QLQ-C30) at **12 weeks** post-randomisation. Secondary: overall survival, toxicity, compliance, QLQ-C30 and QLQ-BR23, and patient satisfaction with cancer care (EORTC PATSAT C33).

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: Both co-primaries read out in favour of adding MDT: PFS separated at **HR 0.48** and the QoL summary score met **non-inferiority** against a -10 point margin. See the figure captions for the arm-level values.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents oligometastatic breast cancer with ≤5 lesions, predominantly ER+/HER2- disease in the first-line setting with 1-3 mostly bony metastases. Does not represent pts with >5 lesions, HER2+ or triple-negative disease in meaningful numbers, or pts whose entire metastatic burden requires palliative rather than ablative RT.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Recruitment **stopped early** at <20% of the initial and <40% of the amended target, leaving **N=87** and a wide HR CI (0.25-0.91). Open-label design with a patient-reported co-primary. **No OS data reported in source**, and the toxicity secondary endpoint is likewise absent from the presented slides.

analysis_sections.label: Discussion
analysis_sections.body: The presenter frames this as the **first RCT showing a PFS benefit from MDT in oligometastatic breast cancer**, extending the STOMP / SABR-COMET line of evidence into a histology where systemic control is comparatively good. Eight further randomised trials are accruing, so the magnitude here should be read as provisional.

open_questions: Does the PFS benefit translate to overall survival
Which oligometastatic breast subgroups benefit most
Optimal RT dose and fractionation for bone-dominant oligomet breast

studies.name: APBI-IMRT Florence
studies.tldr: 15yr IBTR 7.7% APBI vs 4.2% WBI, HR 1.57 (0.82-3.04) p=0.17; excess is new primaries, not true local relapse.
details: 🔍 Phase III equivalence, N=520, 1:1, single-institution Florence, accrual 2005-2013
🔍 Eligibility: post-BCS, pT <25mm, final surgical margins ≥5mm, age >40
📐 Powered on 5yr estimated IBTR 3%, equivalence margin 5%, 80% power
📊 APBI arm 30Gy in 5# IMRT; WBI arm 50Gy in 25# + 10Gy in 5# tumour-bed boost
📊 IBTR split at 15yr: true local relapse 2.1% APBI vs 1.6% WBI (p=0.75); new ipsilateral primary 5.9% vs 2.7% (p=0.09)
📊 Locoregional recurrence 7.2% vs 5.0% (p=0.28); distant mets 2.7% vs 4.6% (p=0.35)
📊 Deaths 21.5% vs 19.6% (p=0.66); breast-cancer deaths 2.3% vs 3.1% (p=0.79)
⚠️ Powered for 5yr equivalence, not 15yr; the IBTR curve keeps diverging past the powered window
⚠️ Local-relapse vs new-primary adjudication is investigator-assigned, not molecularly confirmed
⚠️ Single-institution, 14 withdrew; toxicity/cosmesis reported per-protocol, survival ITT
🔗 Only 5-fraction PBI trial with 15yr data; IMPORT LOW and NSABP B-39 report shorter f/u at larger fraction counts

figures.caption: Phase III, n=520, 1:1 randomisation 2005-2013. PBI 30Gy in 5# vs WBI 50Gy in 25# + 10Gy in 5# TBB.

verdict.rationale: Randomised phase III, prespecified 5yr equivalence endpoint, 15yr mature f/u, no significant divergence on any oncologic endpoint. Reinforces PBI as a listed option.
verdict.audience: Post-BCS early breast cancer, pT <25mm, margins ≥5mm, age >40

studies.significance: The 15yr excess sits in new ipsilateral primaries (5.9% vs 2.7%, p=0.09), not true local relapse (2.1% vs 1.6%). That splits the decision: 30Gy in 5# to the tumour bed holds the bed, but leaves untreated breast tissue at risk, which is a surveillance and endocrine-adherence question rather than an argument for whole-breast coverage.
studies.monday_clinic: In the post-BCS pt over 40 with a pT <25mm tumour and ≥5mm margins, this supports 5-fraction PBI as a durable option out to 15 years; it does not extend to node-positive disease, close margins, or younger pts, and the untreated-breast new-primary rate is the tradeoff to counsel.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Phase III **equivalence** trial, **n=520**, 1:1 randomisation, accrued **2005-2013**, single institution. Powered on a **5-year estimated IBTR of 3%** with a **5% equivalence margin** and 80% power. Survival outcomes analysed **ITT**; toxicity and cosmesis **per protocol** after 14 withdrawals.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Post **breast-conserving surgery**, **pT <25 mm**, **final surgical margins ≥5 mm**, **age >40 years**. Nodal status and receptor subtype not specified in the source slides.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: APBI arm: **30 Gy in 5 fractions IMRT** to the partial breast. WBI arm: **50 Gy in 25 fractions** plus a **10 Gy in 5 fraction tumour-bed boost**. The comparator is a conventionally fractionated whole-breast course, not a modern hypofractionated 26 Gy/5 or 40 Gy/15 schedule.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: **IBTR HR 1.57 (95% CI 0.82-3.04), p=0.17** at 15 years. The gap widens across timepoints (5yr 2.3% vs 1.2%, 10yr 4.2% vs 2.7%, 15yr 7.7% vs 4.2%) but never reaches significance.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: The only 5-fraction PBI dataset reporting to **15 years**. IMPORT LOW and NSABP B-39 support PBI at shorter follow-up with different fractionation, so this trial carries the durability question for the ultrahypofractionated schedule specifically.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents post-BCS early breast cancer with pT <25 mm, margins ≥5 mm, and age over 40. Does not represent node-positive disease, close or positive margins, pts under 40, or tumours ≥25 mm.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Powered for **5-year** equivalence, so the 15-year read is descriptive rather than a met endpoint. Single institution. The **local relapse vs new primary** split that carries the interpretation is investigator-adjudicated, not molecularly confirmed.

open_questions: Does the new-primary excess keep widening past 15 years
Do modern hypofractionated WBI schedules change the comparison

studies.name: DBCG RT Natural
studies.tldr: 5yr invasive LR 1.5% with PBI vs 9.8% randomised no-PBI vs 8.2% self-selected no-PBI; RT+ET arm had zero events.
details: 🔍 Phase III randomised, ≥60yr, pT1N0, unifocal, non-lobular, ER≥10%, HER2 normal, grade 1-2, margin ≥2mm
🔍 PBI 40Gy/15fr vs no PBI; stratified by institution and ET yes/no; third self-selecting no-PBI cohort
🔍 1° EP 5yr invasive local recurrence; powered on assumed 5yr LR 2%, accepted max 4%, target 926 randomised
📊 LR by treatment received (RT per protocol), CIF % (95% CI)
📊 41 local recurrences total, all invasive; 36 isolated, 40 deemed true LR and 1 new primary
📊 39 of 41 LR occurred in pts who had no PBI
📊 Distant failure rare and balanced: 4 events, 2 +RT and 2 no PBI
⚠️ Median f/u 4 years against a 5yr primary endpoint; the 1.5% RT estimate is still immature
⚠️ Third arm is self-selecting, not randomised, so its 8.2% is confounded by choice
⚠️ Trial stopped early by the DMC per planned stopping rules; early stopping inflates effect estimates
🔗 Discussant (Coles): RT or ET give similar local control in low-risk pts; surgery alone carries high LR even here

figures.caption: Trial schema: PBI 40Gy/15fr vs no PBI, target 926 randomised, 1° EP 5yr invasive LR.

verdict.rationale: Randomised, prespecified LR endpoint, stopped early for excess recurrence without PBI. Directly contests RT omission in the ≥60yr low-risk group PRIME II / CALGB 9343 licensed.
verdict.audience: ≥60yr, pT1N0 unifocal non-lobular ER≥10% HER2-normal grade 1-2, margin ≥2mm

studies.significance: The RT read is the 2x2: +RT+ET was 0/105 events while -RT-ET hit 12.2%, and +RT-ET (3.0%) tracked -RT+ET (3.7%). PBI at 40Gy/15fr can therefore substitute for endocrine therapy in a woman who will not take or tolerate ET, rather than only supplement it.
studies.monday_clinic: In a woman ≥60 with pT1N0 grade 1-2 ER+ HER2-normal disease after breast conservation, this questions omitting PBI on PRIME II grounds, particularly where ET adherence is doubtful; it does not speak to lobular histology, ER<10%, or node-positive disease.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Phase III randomised Danish (DBCG) trial of **PBI vs no PBI** after breast conservation, stratified by institution and ET yes/no, with a **third self-selecting no-PBI cohort**. Planned **926 randomised**, interim at 200 pts with 2yr f/u; **median follow-up 4 years** at this first report.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: **≥60 years**, unilateral unifocal **pT1N0** breast cancer, non-lobular, **ER≥10%**, HER2 normal, **grade 1-2**, limited DCIS, **margin ≥2mm**, treated with breast conservation. ET given per DBCG guideline (recommended for pT1c and/or grade 2).

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: **Partial breast irradiation, 40Gy in 15 fractions.** No whole-breast or nodal arm, so the result speaks to PBI specifically, not to omission of comprehensive breast RT.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Primary: 5-year invasive local recurrence**, assumed 2% with an **accepted maximum of 4%**. Secondary: loco-regional side effects and QoL, neither reported in source.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: All 41 recurrences were invasive, 36 isolated, and **39 of 41 occurred in pts without PBI**. Distant failure was rare (4 events, 2 per group). The no-RT arms crossed the prespecified **4% threshold**.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: PRIME II and CALGB 9343 licensed RT omission in older low-risk pts on the strength of low absolute LR with ET, but both assumed ET adherence. Here the **-RT -ET group reached 12.2%**, which is the population omission actually creates when ET is declined or stopped.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents women ≥60 with pT1N0, non-lobular, ER≥10%, HER2-normal, grade 1-2 disease after breast conservation with ≥2mm margins. Does not represent lobular histology, grade 3, node-positive, ER-low or HER2-positive disease, or pts under 60.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: **Median f/u 4 years for a 5-year endpoint**, and the trial was **stopped early** by the DMC, both of which favour an inflated effect estimate. The self-selecting no-PBI cohort is not randomised, and the 2x2 treatment-received analysis is not the randomised comparison. Toxicity and QoL, the secondary endpoints that would price the cost of PBI, are not in source.

analysis_sections.label: Discussion
analysis_sections.body: The discussant framed it as **RT or ET giving similar local control** in low-risk pts, with **surgery alone carrying high LR even here**. That reframes the omission question from "can we drop RT" to "which single adjuvant modality, and what happens when the pt takes neither".

studies.name: EORTC IM-MS (22922/10925)
studies.tldr: 20yr OS 61.0% vs 61.8%, HR 1.00 (0.90-1.10), P=0.967: early 15yr survival signal gone, BCM benefit offset by non-BCM deaths.
details: 🔍 Randomised EORTC 22922/10925, stage I-III breast, ± internal mammary + medial supraclavicular RT; N≈2002 per arm (at-risk table)
📊 Cause-specific mortality at 20y: the benefit and the price (both significant, opposite directions)
📐 A 3.8% absolute BCM reduction is cancelled by a 4.6% absolute non-BCM excess, hence the null OS
⚠️ RT-attributable late toxicity is the counterweight: cardiac disease 15.2% vs 11.7%, lung fibrosis 6.3% vs 3.2%, cardiac fibrosis 2.7% vs 1.7%

🔗 Discussant framing: DBCG IMN2 (2007-2014) mean heart doses were 4-9× lower (MHD 1.2 Gy right-sided, 2.3 Gy left-sided) and IM-MS RT there reduced distant mets, BCM and improved OS in node-positive pts at 15 years
⚠️ Toxicity here reflects 1996-2004 planning; the trial answers whether IM-MS RT helped with that era's heart dose, not with contemporary technique
⚠️ No difference in second cancers or second breast cancers between arms, so the non-BCM excess is not a second-malignancy story

figures.caption: Primary endpoint overall survival rate (ITT) at 20 years: 61.0% versus 61.8%. HR=1.00; 95% CI 0.90-1.10; P=0.967.

verdict.rationale: Randomised, prespecified OS primary, 20y follow-up, now null after a positive 15y read. Divergence is internally valid; era-dependent heart dose is the caveat, not a design flaw.
verdict.audience: Stage I-III breast cancer, incl. pN0, considering IM + medial supraclavicular RT

studies.significance: The mortality split is the RT read: BCM 18.6% vs 22.4% (HR 0.82) bought at a non-BCM cost of 20.4% vs 15.8% (HR 1.26), with cardiac disease 15.2% vs 11.7%. Since DBCG IMN2 heart doses ran 4-9× lower, the target-volume decision hinges on achievable MHD, not on abandoning IM coverage.
studies.monday_clinic: In pN0 stage I-III breast cancer this does not support adding internal mammary and medial supraclavicular coverage (DFS HR 0.93, P=0.318); it does not settle the node-positive question, where modern low heart-dose technique still carries a 15-year benefit signal.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Randomised EORTC phase 3 (22922/10925), stage I-III breast cancer, ± internal mammary and medial supraclavicular irradiation. This is the **20-year** ITT readout, presented as an ESTRO 2026 plenary, with a prespecified **pN0** analysis.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: The randomised question is **target volume**, not dose: adding IM-MS fields to standard breast/chest-wall treatment. Dose and fractionation are **not reported in source**. Mean heart dose is the operative technical variable, and the discussant contrasts it with DBCG IMN2 (**MHD 1.2 Gy right-sided, 2.3 Gy left-sided**, 4-9× lower than in this trial).

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Primary: overall survival (ITT).** Secondary reported here: DFS and DMFS (DATECAN definitions, so DFS includes all deaths and all breast events including DCIS and contralateral), breast-cancer mortality, non-breast-cancer mortality, second cancers, and late RT toxicity.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: OS was flat at 20 years (**HR=1.00, P=0.967**), as were DFS 48.2% vs 49.0% (HR=0.97, P=0.515) and DMFS 58.9% vs 59.8% (HR=0.97, P=0.578). The signal sits in the **competing-mortality split** shown in the table above, not in any composite endpoint.

analysis_sections.label: Safety
analysis_sections.body: Cardiac disease **15.2% vs 11.7%**, lung fibrosis **6.3% vs 3.2%**, cardiac fibrosis **2.7% vs 1.7%**. No statistical difference in second cancers or second breast cancers between arms, so the excess non-breast-cancer mortality tracks with **cardiopulmonary late effects** rather than second malignancy.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: DBCG IMN2 (irradiation 2007-2014) reported reduced distant metastasis, reduced BCM and **improved OS in node-positive** pts at 15 years, with mean heart doses 4-9× lower than here. The two trials disagree on OS, and heart dose is the most plausible discriminator.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents stage I-III breast cancer treated with 1990s-2000s era planning, including a large pN0 population. Does not represent pts treated with contemporary heart-sparing technique at DBCG IMN2-level mean heart doses.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Toxicity and the competing-mortality penalty are **technique-era dependent**; RT dose, fractionation and delivery details are not reported in source. The pN0 analysis is a **subgroup**, though the null OS is the trial-level primary result.

analysis_sections.label: Discussion
analysis_sections.body: The 15-year positive read did not hold to 20 years because non-breast-cancer deaths kept accruing. The clinical question shifts from **whether** IM-MS coverage works (it lowered BCM) to **whether the heart dose it costs can be made small enough** to let that benefit surface in OS.

open_questions: Does IM-MS benefit survive at modern mean heart doses?
Which node-positive subsets justify IM coverage?
Do proton or DIBH techniques eliminate the non-BCM excess?

studies.name: IMPORT HIGH
studies.tldr: 10yr IBTR 3.5% vs 3.7% vs 5.5% across 40+16Gy, 48Gy SIB, 53Gy SIB; further escalation adds nothing.
details: 🔍 Phase 3 RCT, 1:1:1, N=2617 from 76 UK hospitals, accrual 2009-2015
🔍 Eligibility: women ≥18yr, BCS for invasive early breast (pT1-3, pN0-pN3a, M0) requiring tumour bed boost
📊 10-yr IBTR by arm (5-yr rates from Lancet 2023 for reference)
📐 Absolute OS difference at 10yr vs 40Gy/15F: -0.5 (-3.0, 2.8) for 48Gy, 1.5 (-1.4, 5.1) for 53Gy
🔍 All arms delivered in 3 weeks; SIB escalates only the high-risk tumour bed while modestly de-dosing distant whole breast

details.text: 📊 Moderate/marked AEs at 10yr, all randomised groups

details: ⚠️ 53Gy arm numerically worse for IBTR, not better: dose escalation above 48Gy is a dead end here
⚠️ Source gives no HRs or between-arm p-values; non-inferiority read at 10yr is on absolute rates only
🔗 Both 40+16Gy and 48Gy remain below the 5% control-arm estimate used in the original 5-yr sample size calculation
🔍 Annual clinical follow-up to 10yr; PROs and photographs only to 5yr, so late cosmesis beyond 5yr is clinician-scored

verdict.rationale: Randomised 3-arm phase 3, prespecified 10-yr follow-up of a published primary; reaffirms 48Gy/15F SIB rather than changing it. No new comparator.
verdict.audience: Invasive early breast pT1-3 pN0-pN3a M0 post-BCS requiring tumour bed boost

studies.significance: The transferable parameter is 48Gy/15F in 3 weeks with the boost built in, no separate 8-fraction phase, holding IBTR at 3.7% at 10yr with moderate/marked distortion under 18%. The 53Gy arm settles the escalation question: 5.5% IBTR, worse not better, so the decision this moves is boost delivery (SIB vs sequential), not boost dose.
studies.monday_clinic: In pT1-3 pN0-pN3a post-BCS pts you would otherwise book for 40Gy/15F plus a sequential 16Gy boost, this supports a 15-fraction 48Gy SIB as an equivalent-control option; it does not speak to boost omission in low-risk pts, who were not the population studied.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Phase 3 randomised **1:1:1**, **N=2617** across **76 UK hospitals**, accrued 2009-2015. Annual clinical follow-up to **10 years**; PROs and photographs collected only to 5 years.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Women **≥18 years** after breast conserving surgery for invasive early breast cancer, **pT1-3, pN0-pN3a, M0**, all **requiring tumour bed boost RT**. Described by the investigators as a **higher-than-average risk** group.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: Control was **40Gy/15F whole breast plus sequential 16Gy/8F boost**. Both experimental arms used a **simultaneous integrated boost in 15 fractions over 3 weeks**, **48Gy (3.2Gy/F)** or **53Gy (3.5Gy/F)** to the tumour bed, with a modest dose reduction to whole breast distant from the tumour.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Primary: ipsilateral breast tumour relapse (IBTR)**, reported here at 10 years. Secondary reporting covers overall survival and clinician-assessed late normal tissue effects.

analysis_sections.label: Safety
analysis_sections.body: Moderate/marked AEs at 10 years were low across **all randomised groups**: breast distortion/shrinkage **<18%**, induration **<10%**, telangiectasia **<2%**, breast oedema **<2%**. No arm-specific late toxicity split is given in the source.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: At 5 years (Coles, Lancet 2023) IBTR was **1.9% / 2.0% / 3.2%**, and 48Gy was declared non-inferior in absolute terms. The 10-year data preserve that ordering, and both 40+16Gy and 48Gy stay **below the 5% control estimate** assumed in the original sample size calculation.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents boost-indicated invasive early breast cancer after conserving surgery, node-negative through pN3a. Does not represent pts in whom boost would be omitted, DCIS-only disease, or post-mastectomy settings.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Source reports **absolute rates only**, no between-arm HRs or non-inferiority p-values at 10 years. PRO and photographic cosmesis stopped at 5 years, so 10-year toxicity is clinician-scored. **Conference presentation**, not yet a full peer-reviewed 10-year report.

analysis_sections.label: Discussion
analysis_sections.body: The practical yield is logistic as much as oncologic: SIB collapses boost into the same 15 fractions, cutting visits without paying an IBTR or late-toxicity penalty. **Further escalation to 53Gy does not help**, which closes the dose-escalation arm of this question in a boost-indicated population.

open_questions: Does SIB benefit extend to post-mastectomy or nodal RT settings?
Which boost-indicated subgroups could safely omit boost entirely?
Five-fraction SIB as the next de-escalation step?

studies.name: HypoG-01
studies.tldr: 118 first events at 4.8y median f/u; LRR infrequent and mostly in-volume, patterns comparable between 40Gy/15fx and 50Gy/25fx.
details: 🔍 Pre-planned secondary analysis (ITT) of the HypoG-01 phase III trial, N=1,260
🔍 Randomisation: 40 Gy/15 fx (3-week) vs 50 Gy/25 fx (5-week), both + tumour-bed boost
🔍 1° EP of this analysis: first oncological event (LRR, distant recurrence, or second malignancy)
📊 20/30 recurrence sites in-volume (67%); 19/30 nodal sites, mainly L1 & L2
🔍 LRR classified vs CTV per ESTRO guidelines: in-volume, marginal (outside CTV, ≥50% prescribed dose), out-of-volume (<50%)
🔍 Dosimetry: planned dose at the recurrence site estimated on the initial RT-plan CT
⚠️ Arm-by-arm event counts not reported in source (panel B shows 16%/31%/51% split without arm labels legible in OCR)
⚠️ 19-20 LRR events total is too few to exclude a modest arm difference; "not obviously different" is an eyeball read, not a powered comparison
🔗 Consistent with the START/UK FAST-Forward era conclusion that moderate hypofractionation does not shift failure patterns, now extended to a regional-nodal-irradiation-eligible population

figures.caption: 40 Gy/15 fractions vs 50 Gy/25 fractions + tumour-bed boost. 1,260 pts, median follow-up 4.8 years.

verdict.rationale: Pre-planned secondary analysis of a randomised phase III; supports moderate hypofractionation and ESTRO contouring. Event count (19 iLRR) too small for a powered arm comparison.
verdict.audience: Breast cancer receiving adjuvant RT + tumour-bed boost per HypoG-01 eligibility

studies.significance: The contouring read matters more than the fractionation read here: 20/30 recurrence sites fell in-volume and 19/30 nodal recurrences sat in L1-L2, so ESTRO CTV definitions covered where disease actually came back. That supports keeping current axillary level coverage rather than expanding target volumes when moving to 40 Gy/15 fx.
studies.monday_clinic: In breast pts eligible for adjuvant RT with a tumour-bed boost, this supports 40 Gy/15 fx with standard ESTRO-guided CTVs without widening nodal volumes; it does not address ultra-hypofractionation or pts whose recurrence risk sits outside the trial's eligibility.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: **Pre-planned secondary analysis (ITT)** of the HypoG-01 phase III randomised trial. **N=1,260**, median follow-up **4.8 years**.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: Randomisation was **40 Gy/15 fractions over 3 weeks** vs **50 Gy/25 fractions over 5 weeks**, each with a **tumour-bed boost**. Target volumes followed **ESTRO contouring guidelines**.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **First oncological event**: locoregional recurrence (LRR), distant recurrence (DR), or second malignancy. LRR classified against the CTV as **in-volume**, **marginal** (outside CTV, ≥50% prescribed dose), or **out-of-volume** (<50%), with planned dose at the recurrence site re-estimated on the initial RT-plan CT.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: **118 first events**. LRR was the lowest-frequency event type; distant recurrence (n=61) and second malignancy (n=37) dominated. **20/30 recurrence sites were in-volume (67%)** and **19/30 nodal sites** were mainly **levels I and II**.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents breast cancer pts treated with adjuvant RT plus a tumour-bed boost under ESTRO contouring guidelines. Does not represent ultra-hypofractionated (26 Gy/5 fx) schedules or pts treated with non-ESTRO target-volume definitions.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Arm-level event counts are not reported in the source, so the "no obvious difference" claim between arms cannot be checked. With roughly **20 LRR events** total, the analysis is descriptive and underpowered to exclude a modest fractionation effect on failure pattern.

analysis_sections.label: Discussion
analysis_sections.body: The actionable signal is target-volume adequacy rather than fractionation: recurrences clustered **inside** the treated volume and in **axillary levels I-II**, which argues the ESTRO CTV is catching the right anatomy rather than that coverage needs widening. Out-of-volume failures would have been the argument for larger fields; they were the minority.

open_questions: Do failure patterns hold with ultra-hypofractionation (5-fraction) schedules?
Does boost technique alter in-volume vs marginal recurrence distribution?

studies.name: DBCG HYPO
studies.tldr: 10-yr grade 2-3 breast induration 19.5% with 40Gy/15fx vs 24.7% with 50Gy/25fx, HR 0.76, p=0.005; no recurrence or survival cost.
details: 🔍 Phase III randomised non-inferiority, 1:1, Denmark/Norway/Germany, accrual 2009-2014
🔍 N=1,882 women, node-negative breast cancer or DCIS
🔍 Whole-breast RT 50 Gy/25 fx vs 40 Gy/15 fx; no boost or nodal fields described in source
📊 1° EP was 3-yr grade ≥2 breast induration at two consecutive visits or final f/u; morbidity scored yr 0,1,2,3,4,5,10
📐 Median f/u 12.8 yr, longer than most moderate-hypofx breast datasets
📊 10-yr outcomes by fractionation arm
📊 No significant differences in locoregional recurrence, distant failure or breast cancer mortality
⚠️ LRR, distant failure and BCM reported only as 'no significant difference'; event counts and HRs not in source
⚠️ Non-inferiority margin for the recurrence endpoints not stated in source, so the tumour-control read is directional
🔗 Extends START-B and the UK 10-yr hypofx data with prospectively scored induration to 10 yr rather than clinician-recalled fibrosis

verdict.rationale: Prespecified 10-yr analysis of a randomised phase III with 12.8-yr median f/u; reinforces moderate hypofractionation already standard worldwide.
verdict.audience: Node-negative early breast cancer or DCIS after breast-conserving surgery

studies.significance: The induration difference is not just non-inferiority, it favours 40 Gy/15 fx (24.7% vs 19.5%, HR 0.76, p=0.005), so the shorter course is the lower-fibrosis option, not merely the more convenient one. Applies to node-negative and DCIS whole-breast fields; boost and nodal coverage are not addressed in source.
studies.monday_clinic: In node-negative invasive breast cancer or DCIS after lumpectomy, this supports 40 Gy/15 fx over 50 Gy/25 fx on long-term induration as well as convenience; it does not speak to node-positive pts needing regional nodal irradiation.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: **Phase III randomised non-inferiority trial**, 1:1, run across Denmark, Norway and Germany with accrual 2009-2014. **Median follow-up 12.8 years**; these are the prespecified 10-year analyses.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: **1,882 women with node-negative breast cancer or DCIS**. Allocation was 949 to 50 Gy and 933 to 40 Gy after exclusions; 936 and 917 women respectively carried forward in the flow diagram.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: Whole-breast RT only: **50 Gy in 25 fractions vs 40 Gy in 15 fractions**. No boost, nodal target volume, or technique detail (IMRT vs 3D, prone vs supine) is reported in the source.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Primary: grade ≥2 breast induration at 3 years**, scored at two consecutive visits or at final follow-up. Morbidity assessed at years 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 and 10; recurrence and survival were prespecified 10-year secondary analyses.

analysis_sections.label: Safety
analysis_sections.body: Induration is the toxicity endpoint here and it **favours the hypofractionated arm**. No other late-effect domain (cardiac, pneumonitis, cosmesis scoring, second malignancy) is reported in the source.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: Consistent with START-B and the UK long-term hypofractionation experience, but adds **prospectively scored induration out to 10 years** in a trial that also enrolled DCIS.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents node-negative early breast cancer and DCIS treated with whole-breast irradiation alone. Does not represent node-positive disease requiring regional nodal irradiation, post-mastectomy chest wall RT, or ultra-hypofractionated 26 Gy/5 fx schedules.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Locoregional recurrence, distant failure and breast cancer mortality are reported only as **not significantly different**, with no event counts, HRs, or non-inferiority margin in the source. Induration scoring is clinician-assessed and not blinded to fractionation.

open_questions: Does the induration advantage hold with a tumour bed boost?
How does 40 Gy/15 fx compare with 26 Gy/5 fx at 10 years?

⚠️ 10-yr follow-up on a 2012-2016 cohort is model-projected, not fully observed

figures.caption: Study aim: 5- and 10-yr IBTR with/without boost. Assisi thresholds: <3% boost, <6% no boost.

verdict.rationale: Registry cohort with non-random boost allocation; confounding by indication and a 149-pt no-boost ≥3 RF stratum undercut the omission claim.
verdict.audience: Post-BCS invasive breast cancer treated with WBRT, Dutch cohort 2012-2016

studies.significance: The boost arm carries HIGHER crude IBTR at every stratum (10-yr 3.3% vs 2.7% at ≥3 RF), which is confounding by indication, not harm, and it means these data cannot estimate boost efficacy at all. What they do support is a floor: with 0-2 risk factors, 10-yr IBTR is 1.2% either way, so the omission decision rests on that absolute rate.
studies.monday_clinic: In post-BCS pts with 0-2 of the listed risk factors receiving WBRT, this supports discussing boost omission on absolute-risk grounds; it does not inform pts with ≥3 risk factors, where the ≥3 RF no-boost stratum is only 149 pts.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Dutch population-based cohort (DBRT / Netherlands Cancer Registry linkage) of breast-conserving treatment, **2012-2016**. **Non-randomised**: boost use reflects clinician risk assessment, not allocation.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Breast conserving treatment with or without an RT boost. Strata by **number of risk factors**: 0-2 (15,085 no boost / 13,845 boost), ≥3 (149 / 733), uncertain (592 / 944).

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: Whole-breast RT with or without tumour bed boost. **No dose, fractionation, boost technique, or target-volume detail reported in source.**

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Primary:** ipsilateral breast tumour recurrence (IBTR), histologically confirmed via a pathology-report text/code algorithm. Cumulative incidence reported at **5 and 10 years** by risk-factor count.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: **Low IBTR across every subgroup.** Only the ≥3 risk-factor boost group crossed an Assisi threshold at 10 years (**3.3%**, vs the <3% bar).

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: **EORTC 22881-10882** anchored the ~50% relative IBTR reduction from boost. Absolute IBTR here is an order of magnitude below that trial's era, which is the authors' argument that a **relative** halving now buys little **absolute** benefit.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents pts having BCS + WBRT for invasive breast cancer with 0-2 of the five listed risk factors, treated in the modern systemic-therapy era. Does not represent pts with ≥3 risk factors, DCIS-only, or anyone whose boost benefit would be estimated from these data, since allocation was not randomised.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: **Confounding by indication** is the dominant issue: higher-risk pts got the boost, so crude boost-arm rates run higher. The ≥3 RF no-boost cell is **149 pts**. 10-yr estimates on a 2012-2016 cohort are **projected**, and IBTR ascertainment was algorithmic.

open_questions: Which ≥3 risk-factor subgroups actually gain from a boost
Does boost omission hold with longer observed follow-up
Boost value in pts with residual disease after neoadjuvant therapy

studies.name: RCC SBRT local control
studies.tldr: 100% local control at 5 years for primary RCC treated with SBRT, per a conference tweet with no denominator.
details: 📊 Claim in source: 100% local control at 5 yrs for RCC treated with SBRT
⚠️ No N, no dose/fractionation, no tumor size or stage reported in source tweet
⚠️ Tweet carries no trial name, NCT, or study design; likely a conference slide reaction post
🔍 Source is a celebratory practitioner tweet (@TylerSbrt), not a data-bearing abstract or figure
🔗 Prior primary-RCC SABR data (IROCK pooled series, FASTRACK II) report local control in the ~97-100% range, so the direction is consistent, but the specific series here is unidentifiable
⚠️ Local control in primary RCC is defined heterogeneously across series (radiographic progression vs any growth); a 100% figure without the definition is not interpretable
⚠️ No cancer-specific survival, renal-function, or toxicity data in source, so the trade-off that actually gates SBRT vs partial nephrectomy or ablation is absent

verdict.rationale: Single tweet with a bare local-control percentage; no N, design, dose, or follow-up definition to classify against. Underlying series not identifiable from source.

studies.significance: The RT-relevant gap is technique: no dose, fractionation, or tumor-size cap is in the source, and those are what determine whether a primary-RCC SBRT local-control figure transfers to a reader's practice. Without them this does not move the SBRT vs partial nephrectomy or ablation decision.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: **Not reported in source.** The tweet gives no phase, sample size, sites, or accrual period; the underlying series cannot be identified from the text provided.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: **No dose, fractionation, or technique reported in source.** Primary-RCC SBRT regimens vary widely across published series, so the local-control claim cannot be attributed to a specific regimen.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: **100% local control at 5 years** is the only figure in the source. No denominator, no confidence interval, and no local-control definition accompany it.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Single social-media claim without a linked abstract, figure, or registration. Local control alone omits the renal-function and cancer-specific-survival endpoints that gate modality choice in primary RCC.

SBRT vs partial nephrectomy or thermal ablation in operable primary RCC

sites.intro: OligoCare's 3yr in-field failure ranges 8.1% prostate to 19.6% CRC, so histology, not a single SABR local-control number, should set expectations.

studies.name: OligoCare
studies.tldr: Real-world SABR local in-field progression 5.0% at 1yr, 11.4% at 3yrs across 2447 pts / 3533 lesions; CRC worst.

details.text: 🔍 EORTC OligoCare prospective registry, July 2019 to July 2025, 57 institutions

details: 📊 Lesion distribution: lung 807 (23%), non-vertebral bone 869 (25%), non-regional nodes 558 (16%)
📊 Also spine 515, liver 306 (9%), brain 231 (7%), other 247 (7%)
📊 Primary tumor mix: prostate 1021 (42%), NSCLC 530 (22%), colorectal 518 (21%), breast 378 (15%)
📐 Cumulative incidence of local in-field progression 11.4% (99% CI 10.0-12.9%) at 36 mo
🔍 Minimum PTV dose correlated with outcome; presenters call it the most critical technical factor
🔍 De novo OMD showed better local control than repeat OMD, attributed by authors to higher delivered dose
⚠️ Registry, not randomised: no comparator arm, dose/fractionation heterogeneous across 57 centres, indication bias likely
⚠️ Per-lesion vs per-patient denominators mixed in source (2447 pts, 3533 lesions); local failure rates reported per patient
⚠️ CRC failure despite the highest median dose per fraction in the cohort; radioresistance, not underdosing, is the authors' read
🔗 CRC 3yr in-field failure 19.6% is roughly double prostate 8.1%, so a single 'oligomet SABR' local-control number misleads by histology

figures.caption: 57 institutions, 2447 eligible pts, 3533 lesions. Median age 69 (28-94), 69% male. Median f/u 31 mo.

verdict.rationale: Large prospective multi-site registry with explicit histology-stratified analyses, but non-randomised and no comparator; supports rather than tests current oligomet SABR practice.
verdict.audience: Oligometastatic solid tumors treated with SABR; prostate/NSCLC/CRC/breast…

studies.significance: The histology split is the actionable read: CRC 3yr in-field failure 19.6% vs prostate 8.1%, despite CRC receiving the highest median dose per fraction. That argues for dose escalation or a combination strategy in CRC mets specifically, and it puts minimum PTV dose (not prescription dose) on the plan-review checklist.
studies.monday_clinic: In a CRC oligomet being planned for SABR, this registry supports treating minimum PTV dose as the coverage constraint to scrutinize and sets a realistic ~1 in 5 three-year in-field failure expectation; it does not extend to unirradiated or non-oligometastatic disease.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: EORTC **OligoCare** prospective real-world registry of SABR for oligometastatic disease, **57 institutions**, accrual July 2019 to July 2025. Interim analysis; **median follow-up 31 months** (minimum 6).

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: **2447 eligible pts** with **3533 lesions**. Median age **69** (28-94), **69% male**. Primary tumors: prostate 1021 (42%), NSCLC 530 (22%), colorectal 518 (21%), breast 378 (15%).

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: SABR to metastatic sites; lesion locations were lung 807 (23%), non-vertebral bone 869 (25%), non-regional lymph node 558 (16%), spine 515, liver 306 (9%), brain 231 (7%), other 247 (7%). **Minimum PTV dose** correlated with outcome and is named the most critical technical factor; prescription dose and fractionation not reported in source.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: Local in-field progression reported as cumulative incidence. No primary endpoint stated in the source; no survival or systemic-progression endpoints given here.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: Local in-field progression **5.0% at 1 year** and **11.4% (99% CI 10.0-12.9%) at 3 years**, i.e. **88.6% local control at 3 years**, 237 events among 2447 pts.

analysis_sections.label: Discussion
analysis_sections.body: Colorectal primaries failed most (**19.6% at 3 years**) despite the highest median dose per fraction, which the authors read as relative radioresistance rather than underdosing and a case for **dose escalation or combination** approaches. **De novo** oligometastatic disease outperformed **repeat** OMD, attributed to higher delivered dose.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents pts receiving SABR for oligometastatic disease from prostate, NSCLC, colorectal, or breast primaries across European practice. Does not represent randomised comparison of SABR against systemic therapy alone, nor histologies outside the four dominant primaries.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Registry design with **no comparator arm** and heterogeneous dose/fractionation across 57 centres; indication and selection bias are unaddressed in source. Safety and toxicity outcomes are **not reported** in the source content.

open_questions: Optimal dose escalation strategy for colorectal oligometastases
Minimum PTV dose threshold for durable local control
Whether repeat OMD failure reflects dose or biology

analysis_sections.label: Results

Background
Metastasis-free survival (MFS) has been validated as a surrogate endpoint for overall survival in non-metastatic prostate
cancer, and may represent an adaptable and clinically meaningful endpoint in oligorecurrent disease.
The phase II RADIOSA trial compared stereotactic body radiotherapy (SBRT) alone (Arm A) versus SBRT plus 6-month
androgen deprivation therapy (ADT, Arm B). This post-hoe analysis evaluated MFS and eugonadal MFS after
testosterone recovery, with a visual mapping of disease trajectories through a Sankey diagram.
Methods
A total of 102 patients were randomized 1:l to Arm A (SBRT) or Arm B (SBRT + ADT).
Median follow-up (Reverse KM) was 49.23 months (95% C1 42.47-54.8).
MFS was defined as the time between randomisation and the appearance of a metastatic recurrence (any MI) on imaging.
Eugonadal MFS was calculated from testosterone recovery to new metastasis or last follow-up.
Kaplan-Meier (KM) survival curves were compared with log-rank tests; hazard ratios were estimated using Cox models.

Study aim
Given that a boost reduces the risk of IBTR by approximately 50%' and guided by the Assisi
thresholds?, the objective of our study is:
To determine 5-and 10-year IBTR rates in patients treated with or without a radiotherapy boost
To identify subgroups of patients in whom a boost may be omitted, i.e.
• Treated with a boost who have 10-year IBTR rates of <3%
• Treated without a boost who have 10-year IBTR rates of <6%

Primary outcome
Dutch breast cancer patients treated
with
• Breast conserving treatment
• With or without radiotherapy
boost
• 2012-2016

algorithm based on codes and text in pathology reports
• Cumulative incidence of IBTR is presented according to the presence
of risk factors

Conclusions
• Low IBTR rates across all patient subgroups
• Based on the presence of combined risk factors, 5-and 10-year IBTR rates exceeded
the Assisi thresholds only in:
• Patients with ≥ 3 risk factors treated with a boost at 10 years
Take home messages
• The low IBTR rates across all subgroups suggest that
• In patients with 0-2 risk factors a tumour bed boost can be omitted, in the modern
systemic therapy era
• In patients with ≥ 3 risk factors the clinical relevance of a tumour bed boost remains
open to discussion
• Further research is needed to identify specific subgroups of patients who benefit from a
boost

SBRT vs 5f prostate and pelvic nodal SBRT
Conclusion & Take-Home Message
5 fraction prostate and pelvic nodal (PPN) SBRT is feasible and safe in a
multicentre setting
Compared with prostate-only SBRT, PPN-SBRT increases GI side effects
in the short-term, but symptoms resolved quickly with no difference at 12
weeks
Differences in GI side effects were reflected in patient-reported outcomes
(EPIC-26 bowel domain) at 4 weeks
No differences in acute GU side effects (clinician or patient-reported)
were seen
Ongoing follow-up will provide data on late effects and cancer control

Background & Methods

Background
Methods
• Some breast-cancer patients still develop locoregional or
distant recurrences, or a second malignancy.
• Mapping patterns of oncological failure helps refine therapy
and target-volume definition.
• Pre-planned secondary analysis of the HypoG-01 phase III

Gy/25 fractions) RT + tumour-bed boost

(LRR), distant recurrence (DR) or second malignancy

- In-volume: within CTV
- Marginal: outside CTV, 250% prescribed dose
- Out-of-volume: <50% prescribed dose
• Dosimetry: planned dose at recurrence site estimated on the
initial RT-plan CT scan

Background & Methods
Background
Moderately hypofractionated adjuvant whole-breast radiotherapy for early
node-negative breast cancer is standard worldwide, but long-term
randomised evidence remains limited. Here, we present the Danish
Breast Cancer Group HYPO trial prespecified 10-year outcomes.
Methods
• Phase III randomised non-inferiority trial (1:1) across Denmark,
Norway and Germany (2009-2014)
• 1,882 women with node-negative breast cancer or DCIS
• Whole-breast RT: 50 Gy/25 fractions vs 40 Gy/15 fractions
• Primary endpoint: 3-year grade 22 breast induration at two consequtive
visits, or at final follow-up
• Morbidity evaluated year 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 10
• These results are the prespecified 10-year analyses of toxicity.

• Numerous trials are still ongoing (incl. TAORMINA, STEREO-SEIN, LARA,

SUMMARY
• POP-RT (2 years ADT) demonstrated significant benefit in bFFS, cFFS, and MFS with WPRT.
• PEACE-2 (3 years ADT, modern imaging, dose-escalated RT) showed no significant improvement in bPFS, MFS, CSS, or OS.
Only a borderline trend in cPFS (HR 0.81, p=0.09). No additional toxicity observed.
